meta.title: tinyq
meta.subtitle: Design proposal

banner.text: Everything here sits downstream of assumption A1: at-least-once delivery is acceptable to every consumer. A1 still needs an explicit yes from the platform team. If it falls, this becomes an exactly-once design with a very different ledger.

meta.diagramCaption: The highlighted box is the durable piece: a job is safe once the log accepts it. The dispatcher and the workers are stateless and rebuilt from the log.
meta.timingsCaption: Log scale. The budget is one enqueue round-trip under 25ms; everything else happens off the request path.
meta.footerNote: numbers marked (E) are estimates pending the spikes
meta.canonical: This file is the canonical structured register. Verbatim question rounds live in qa-log.json; prose that does not fit structure lives in NOTES.md; design-doc.html renders from this file.

tldr: One durable append-only job log per tenant, backed by [SQLite](https://sqlite.org): an enqueue acks after a group-committed WAL flush, ~2ms p50 (E).
Workers lease jobs over long-poll HTTP; a lease that expires re-queues its job, so delivery is at-least-once and producers set idempotency keys (A1).
Completed jobs archive to object storage after 24h; the log stays small, and restore is replay from the archive plus the live tail.

constraints.t: A job, once acked, survives the loss of any single machine.
Consumers tolerate duplicate delivery; they do not tolerate loss.

terms.v: One unit of work: an opaque payload plus a queue name and an idempotency key chosen by the producer.
A worker's temporary claim on a job. It expires at a deadline unless renewed; expiry re-queues the job.
A small time-boxed experiment that turns an estimate marked (E) into a measured number.

footnotes.b: The enqueue ack fires after the job row and its queue-position row commit in one SQLite transaction and the WAL flush for that batch returns. One flush loop covers all of a tenant's queues (group commit), so the flush cost amortizes under load.

Lease expiry is checked by the dispatcher's timer wheel, not by workers: a worker that dies silently just stops renewing, and the job re-queues at the deadline. The idempotency key is what makes the resulting duplicate safe for the consumer to detect.

Archival rewrites completed jobs into daily object-storage segments readable with `sqlite3` directly. Restore is: fetch segments, replay the live tail, rebuild queue positions. Spike V2 measures the replay rate.

b: Every queue is a table in one SQLite file per tenant. An enqueue is one transaction: insert the job, insert its queue position, commit; the ack returns once the batch's WAL flush lands[^1].
The log is the only durable state. Dispatcher memory and worker leases can be rebuilt from it after a crash, which keeps recovery boring: reopen the file, replay unexpired leases, resume[^3].
Workers long-poll for jobs and hold a lease per job. Renewals extend the deadline; an expired lease re-queues the job at the head[^2].
Delivery is at-least-once by construction (A1). Producers set an idempotency key, and the archive keeps completed keys for 7 days so consumers can cheaply detect a replay.

paths.name: Enqueue (acked)
segs: insert job + position; group-committed WAL flush
HTTP response on the same connection
paths.note: The flush dominates; everything else is bookkeeping. Numbers pending spike V1.

paths.name: Dispatch to a waiting worker
segs: lease row commit; the deadline starts
payload over the worker's open long-poll
paths.note: A worker is usually already parked on the queue, so dispatch is a push, not a poll. Numbers pending spike V1.

numbers.sub: Sustained rates one tenant is sized for; latency for the same flows is under Request paths.
numbers.note: Per tenant. Both (E) columns are estimates pending spike V1; the ceilings table below says what happens when a rate is exceeded.

ceilings: enqueue rejected past a cap until archival catches up
ceilingsNote: Every ceiling degrades to rejection or lag; none of them corrupts the log.

decisions.r: One file per tenant, one table per queue. Tenants never share a WAL, so one noisy tenant cannot slow another's flush.
decisions.x: Rejected one shared file (cross-tenant flush coupling) and one file per queue (thousands of open files for little isolation gain).

decisions.r: At-least-once with producer idempotency keys, per A1. The archive keeps completed keys for 7 days so consumers can detect duplicates.
decisions.x: Exactly-once was rejected: it moves a dedup ledger into the hot path, and the consumers already tolerate duplicates.

decisions.r: Long-poll HTTP chosen for dispatch; every worker runtime already speaks it.

decisions.t: Worker transport: long-poll HTTP, renewals TBD
decisions.r: Long-poll dispatch stays. Whether lease renewals ride the same connection or a separate endpoint is still open, pending spike V3.

1.q: What owns durability, and what do consumers get?
1.a: One SQLite file per tenant; at-least-once with idempotency keys.
1.n: Both answers matched the recommended option.

2.a: Long-poll HTTP; where renewals live is still open.

assumptions.b: Every current consumer either dedups on an idempotency key or is naturally idempotent. Needs an explicit yes from the platform team before v1.
assumptions.n: If this falls, the design gains an exactly-once ledger in the hot path and most of the simplicity argument goes with it.

assumptions.b: A job must survive the loss of any one machine. Multi-region durability is explicitly out of scope for v1.
Workers renew leases honestly and crash rather than hang in ways that hold a lease forever. The lease deadline is the backstop, not the norm.
Larger payloads go to object storage with a pointer in the job.
assumptions.n: Revised once, 2026-07-21: the original 8 KB bound fell at the first real consumer survey.

open.t: Confirm A1 with every consuming team, in writing.
Does anyone need scheduled (delayed) jobs in v1?
Measure enqueue and dispatch latency under 1k concurrent producers.
Measure restore-from-archive replay rate on a 10 GB log.
Lease renewals on the dispatch connection vs. a separate endpoint.

findings: Lease expiry during archival can double-deliver without a completed-key check
Backpressure 429 needs a retry-after header or producers stampede
